chart.noQuarterlyData: No quarterly data available for this period.
chart.pfcfAnnualOnlyTag: 4 annual years (max available for this ticker)
chart.pfcfNoData: Not enough history to compute P/FCF over this period.
chart.insufficientHistory: Not enough history to plot a reliable trend.
chart.pfcfVerdictBelow: The latest multiple ({{latest}}×) is below the historical median ({{median}}×), attractive relative valuation.
chart.pfcfVerdictAbove: The latest multiple ({{latest}}×) is above the historical median ({{median}}×), stretched vs its own past.
chart.croceSub: Formula: FCF (SBC-adjusted) ÷ (Assets − CurLiab − Goodwill − Excess cash), Bettin/Mauboussin. Excess cash = max(0, cash − 2% × revenue).
chart.croceNoData: Not enough history to compute Cash ROCE over this period (needs positive FCF + positive capital employed over ≥ 4 quarters / 2 fiscal years).
chart.croceVerdictHigh: Cash ROCE durably above 15% ({{n}}/{{total}} periods), consistent operating value creation.
chart.croceVerdictMid: Cash ROCE above the threshold in {{n}}/{{total}} periods, average quality, watch closely.
chart.croceVerdictLow: Cash ROCE rarely above 15% ({{n}}/{{total}} periods), weak operating value creation.

netMargin.why: A positive net margin proves the company actually makes money once all its costs are paid, the foundation of any quality investment.
netMargin.how: Net income ÷ revenue (trailing 12 months). Benchmark: > 5% = good, ≤ 0 = loss-making.

revenueGrowth5y.why: Revenue that grows over time is the engine of value creation, without growth, compounding is hard.
revenueGrowth5y.how: Trend (regression) of trailing revenue over ~5 years. Benchmark: > 10%/year = good.

fcfGrowth5y.why: Free cash flow PER SHARE is what truly enriches the shareholder: it accounts for dilution (share issuance). A company can grow while diluting, here we look at what actually accrues to you.
fcfGrowth5y.how: Trend of adjusted FCF (free cash minus stock-based compensation) divided by share count, over 5 years. Benchmark: > 10%/year = good.

fcfMargin.why: Measures how much truly free cash each euro of sales generates, AFTER accounting for stock-based compensation (often hidden). This is the cash that can be returned or reinvested.
fcfMargin.how: (Operating cash flow − stock-based compensation − capital expenditures) ÷ revenue. Benchmark: > 10% = good.

operatingLeverage.why: Margins that improve as revenue rises reveal a scale advantage: the company becomes more profitable as it grows.
operatingLeverage.how: Slope of the operating margin (trailing 12 months) over ~5 years. Rising = good.

cashRoce.why: Return on the capital actually employed, the core of business quality (Buffett/Bettin-Mauboussin approach). Above the cost of capital (~8-10%), the company creates wealth; below it, it destroys wealth.
cashRoce.how: Adjusted FCF ÷ (assets − current liabilities − goodwill − excess cash). Benchmark: > 15% = good, > 25% = excellent.

netDebtFcf.why: Shows how many years of free cash it would take to repay the debt, a good proxy for financial risk and fragility in a downturn.
netDebtFcf.how: (Total debt − cash) ÷ FCF. Benchmark: < 3 = healthy, > 5 = stretched.

cashConversion.why: Checks that accounting earnings actually convert into real cash. A persistent gap signals "paper" profits (rising receivables, bloating inventories…).
cashConversion.how: Ratio of earnings to cash generated (derived from the P/E and P/FCF). Benchmark: > 1 = cash keeps up with (or exceeds) earnings.

ccc.why: Days between buying inventory and collecting from customers, net of the supplier payment delay. Measures working-capital efficiency and business-model quality. A negative CCC (float model) means customers finance the operations, the operational Holy Grail.
ccc.how: CCC = DSO + DIO − DPO (in days). DSO = receivables/revenue × 365, DIO = inventory/cost of revenue × 365, DPO = payables/cost of revenue × 365. Trend over 5 years: compression = positive signal, lengthening = red flag.

pfcf.why: The price paid for €1 of annual free cash: the most telling valuation measure. An excellent company bought too dear is still a poor investment at the wrong time.
pfcf.how: Market capitalization ÷ FCF (trailing 12 months). Benchmark: < 25× = reasonable, > 35× = expensive.

valuation.why: Gives an indicative buy price to target a desired return: the right "entry timing." This criterion does NOT factor into the quality score, a good company stays good, it's just not yet at the right price.
valuation.how: Discounting of future cash (DCF) based on the target return, the growth assumption and the exit multiple.

shareCount5y.why: The share count decides your slice of the pie: net buybacks concentrate value, dilution disperses it. A compounder buys back or stays flat.
shareCount5y.how: CAGR of diluted share count over ~5 years. Benchmark: declining = buybacks (good), flat = neutral, rising = dilution.

valuation.note: Judged separately from the quality score. The recommended price is the entry point to aim for your target return.
valuation.unavailable: Valuation unavailable (FCF per share not computable for this stock).
valuation.sectorMedian: The median P/FCF of the “{{sector}}” sector is {{value}}×.

chart.title: Cash Conversion Cycle

chart.approxNote: Cost of revenue is not published for this stock, DIO/DPO are computed via revenue (less precise in absolute terms, but the trend remains reliable).

items.q: How is the quality score computed?
items.a: On 10 objective financial criteria: profitability, sales growth, per-share earnings growth, expanding margins, return on invested capital, leverage under control, earnings-to-cash conversion, and collection cycle. Each criterion is validated (YES / PARTIAL / NO) against thresholds drawn from the financial literature (Buffett, Bettin-Mauboussin, Damodaran). The final score is the total.

Over 7,000 names tracked: NYSE & Nasdaq (US), Stoxx 600 and national European indices, major international markets. Free gives à-la-carte analysis on all of them; Pro unlocks full Europe and International data with complete quarterly histories.
No. Lubin Investment is a decision-support tool for retail investors. We do not provide personalized investment advice. Investment decisions remain your sole responsibility.

items.q: How do I cancel my subscription?
items.a: At any time from your account, one click. No commitment, no questions asked. Pro access stays active until the end of the current period.

items.q: What payment methods do you accept?
items.a: Credit card (Visa, Mastercard, Amex), Apple Pay and Google Pay via Stripe. Secure payments, never stored on our servers.

items.q: Can I switch from monthly to yearly?
items.a: Yes, anytime from your account. The change is immediate with prorated adjustment.

hero.title: 10 criteria, public sources, zero black box.
hero.lede: Lubin Investment applies the same lens to every company: ten fundamental criteria, scored from each company's public official accounts. Here's exactly how the engine works.

why.title: Why this method
paragraphs: Most stock analyses rely on opinions, analyst consensus or momentum indicators. These signals change constantly and reflect mostly the market's mood. We chose to anchor on verifiable accounting facts, taken directly from the official accounts each company publishes.

The ten selected criteria cover three dimensions of a quality company: growth (revenue, FCF per share, margins), capital profitability (Cash ROCE, FCF margin) and balance-sheet strength (leverage, cash conversion, operating cycle). It's the same framework that the best long-term investors, Buffett, Mauboussin, Terry Smith, have used for decades, translated into reproducible formulas.

Each criterion answers a simple question, with a clear threshold. No opaque score, no secret weighting. If a company fails a criterion, we tell you which one and why. And if you disagree with one of our thresholds, you can knowingly ignore it.

criteres.pourquoi: A company that doesn't make money isn't an investment case. This first filter eliminates structurally loss-making companies.
Revenue growth remains the best driver of long-term value creation. We measure the 5-year trend to smooth out exceptional years.

criteres.formule: Cash per share growth over 5 years
criteres.pourquoi: What really matters to you as a shareholder is the cash generated per share. We strip out the shares handed to employees, which shrink your slice.

criteres.formule: Annual change in diluted share count over 5 years
criteres.pourquoi: Dilution is when a company issues new shares: your slice of the pie shrinks. The best companies do the opposite, buying back shares instead of issuing them.

criteres.formule: Cash margin = free cash flow / revenue
criteres.pourquoi: Free cash flow is the money actually left in the bank once everything is paid, far more reliable than accounting earnings. A high margin means each euro of revenue generates real cash.
Margins rising over the years reveal a real advantage: the company can raise prices or produce more cheaply. A sign of durable quality.

criteres.formule: Cash generated per €100 invested in the business (Cash ROCE)
criteres.pourquoi: How much cash the company generates for each euro actually invested in its business. Above 15 %, it puts its money to work very efficiently.
How many years of cash it would take to repay all the debt. Above 3 years, the risk becomes serious if things turn sour.
Checks that reported profits turn into real money, not just an accounting entry. A ratio durably below 1 is a warning sign.

criteres.formule: Days the company's cash stays tied up in its cycle (customers, inventory, suppliers)
criteres.pourquoi: The number of days cash is tied up between paying suppliers and getting paid by customers. Short or negative is excellent: its suppliers finance its growth (Apple, Amazon).

limites.title: Limits, what the method does not do

items.title: The method is retrospective.
items.text: It judges a company's historical quality. A quality compounder can perfectly well disappoint in the years to come, and a mediocre company can turn around.

items.title: It ignores valuation.
items.text: The 10 criteria say nothing about the price paid. An excellent company bought too expensive remains a bad investment. We separately publish valuation indicators (P/E, FCF yield, EV/EBITDA).

items.title: It's unsuitable for some sectors.
items.text: Banks, insurance, listed real estate (REITs), pre-profitability companies: net margin, Cash ROCE or CCC criteria don't apply as-is.

items.title: The data isn't perfect.
items.text: Some of it comes from reports the companies filled in poorly. We fix issues continuously, but residual errors can remain.

items.title: No method replaces your judgment.
items.text: Scores are a decision-support tool, not a buy recommendation. You remain the sole judge.
